title: Why Liquidity Is More Than Just One Number in Markets
summary: Market selloffs are often blamed on liquidity, but liquidity isn't a single number. It's a structure with two sides: the amount of money available and its cost. Understanding this can help investors navigate market volatility.
body:
Every time the market takes a dive, the blame often falls on liquidity. People point to various indicators like the Federal Reserve's balance sheet, M2 money supply, credit spreads, and real yields. But which one is the real signal?


According to Delphi Digital, none of these indicators alone fully capture liquidity. Liquidity is not just one number but a complex structure with two key components: the quantity of money available and the cost of accessing it. This dual nature means that simply looking at one metric, like the Fed's balance sheet, doesn't give the full picture.


Understanding liquidity in this way matters because it affects how investors and traders navigate market volatility. If the cost of money rises or the supply tightens, it can lead to selloffs. Recognizing these dynamics can help individuals make more informed decisions about when to buy or sell assets.


To stay ahead, keep an eye on both the quantity and cost of money in the market. Watch for changes in the Federal Reserve's policies, shifts in the M2 money supply, and movements in real yields. These indicators can provide clues about potential market movements and help you make better investment decisions. Read more → https://x.com/Delphi_Digital/status/2056774152191926441
